text: Hyponatremia (serum sodium ≤135 mmol/L) occurs in approximately 13% of nonelite marathon runners.
Critical hyponatremia (serum sodium ≤120 mmol/L) occurs in approximately 0.6% of nonelite marathon runners.
Weight gain during a marathon is strongly associated with hyponatremia, with an odds ratio of 4.2, indicating overhydration as a primary risk factor.
Marathon finishing time greater than 4 hours is strongly associated with hyponatremia compared to finishing under 3:30, with an odds ratio of 7.4.
Consuming more than 3 liters of fluid during a marathon is associated with hyponatremia on univariate analysis.
Drinking fluids at every mile during a marathon is associated with hyponatremia, suggesting runners should drink to thirst rather than on a fixed schedule.
Body-mass-index extremes (both high and low) are independently associated with hyponatremia in marathon runners.
The type of fluid consumed (e.g., sports drink vs. water) and use of NSAIDs were not independently associated with hyponatremia after multivariate adjustment.
